macro_segments.title: The Classification of Oaths: False Oaths vs. Oaths in Vain and Their Respective Prohibitions

micro_segments.title: Introduction to the Two Main Categories of Oaths: Vows (Nedarim) vs. Oaths (Shvuot) and Their Fundamental Differences
The Distinction Between Past-Oriented False Oaths and Future-Oriented Oaths: Which Prohibition is Transgressed
The Opinion That Past Oaths Are Oaths in Vain (Shvuat Shav) and Future Oaths Violate the Prohibition of Disregarding One's Words (Lo Yachel Devaro)
The Alternative Opinion: Past False Oaths (Shvuat Sheker) Incur Different Lashes, Future Oaths Follow Lo Yachel Devaro
The Rambam's Position: All False Oaths—Whether Past or Future—Constitute Shvuat Sheker

macro_segments.title: Textual Source and Derivation: How Oaths in Vain are Derived from Scriptural Prohibition and Cleansing Through Lashes

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Pappa's Statement on the Inference of the Second Opinion from Rabbi Yochanan's Teachings
The General Principle of Prohibitions Without Action: Exceptions Including Oaths, Temurah, and Curses
The Source for Lashes on Oaths in Vain: The Pasuk's Double Use of 'Lashav' and Divine Cleansing
The Debate Between Rav Pappa and Abaye: Whether the Pasuk Means Absolute Non-Forgiveness or Permits Court-Administered Cleansing
The Extension of the Double 'Lashav' to Include False Oaths (Shvuat Sheker), Not Just Oaths in Vain

macro_segments.title: Determining Which False Oath (Past or Future) Incurs Lashes: The Case Analysis of Rabbi Abahu

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Abahu's Question: Which Type of False Oath (Shvuat Sheker) Actually Incurs Lashes from the Court
Eliminating the Future-Oriented Oath That Results in Action: It Cannot Be the Case in Question Since It Would Always Incur Lashes
Eliminating the Future-Oriented Passive Oath: No Lashes Are Incurred When One Passively Violates a Future Oath
The Taught Case of Passively Not Eating: Rabbi Yochanan and Resh Lakish's Agreement That No Lashes Are Due When Time Passes
Rabbi Yochanan's Rationale: Passive Violation of a Prohibition Without Action Exempts from Lashes
Resh Lakish's Alternative Rationale: Doubtful Warning (Hatra'at Safek) at Day's End Exempts from Lashes
Conclusion: The Only Case of False Oath Incurring Lashes Is Past-Oriented (Achalti Velo Achalti)

macro_segments.title: Rava's Explanation: Why Past False Oaths Resemble Oaths in Vain and Merit Lashes

micro_segments.title: Rava's Assertion: The Torah Doubled 'Lashav' to Indicate That False Oaths About the Past Are Like Oaths in Vain
The Conceptual Parallel: Both Oaths in Vain and Past False Oaths Involve the Speech Act Itself Being the Transgression, Not Future Actions
Distinguishing Past False Oaths from Future Oaths: The Violation Occurs Immediately Upon Speaking, Not Upon Future Non-Compliance

macro_segments.title: The Mishna on Future Oaths: Identifying Shvuat Bituy and Its Penalties for Intentional vs. Unintentional Violation

micro_segments.title: The Mishna's Case: Repeated Future Oaths Not to Eat a Specific Loaf and the Rule Against Multiple Oaths on the Same Matter
The Definition of Shvuat Bituy: Future-Oriented Oaths Incurring Lashes If Willful and a Sliding-Scale Offering (Olah Veyored) If Unintentional
The Contrasting Case of Oaths in Vain: Lashes for Willful Violation But No Offering When Done Unintentionally
The Interpretive Problem: Whether the Mishna's Two Clauses Exclude Past-Oriented Oaths from Liability, and Whether Both Exclude Them for the Same Reason

macro_segments.title: Resolving the Mishna's Structure: Past Oaths and the Dispute Between Rabbi Akiva and Rabbi Yishmael Regarding Offerings

micro_segments.title: The First Clause's Exclusion: Rabbi Yishmael's Position That Past-Oriented Oaths Do Not Require an Offering
The Second Clause's Apparent Contradiction: Seeming to Exclude Past Oaths from Lashes When Explicitly False
Resolution: Both Clauses Follow Rabbi Akiva's View That Past False Oaths Always Incur Lashes
The Correct Interpretation of Exclusions: First Clause Excludes From Offering (Not Lashes); Second Excludes From Lashes for Non-False Oaths
The Refined Understanding: Both Clauses Refer to Future Oaths; The Debate Concerns Whether Active vs. Passive Violation Merits Both Lashes and Offering

macro_segments.title: The Requirement of a Minimum Measure (Kezayit) for Transgression: Rabbi Akiva's Challenge to the Standard Rule

micro_segments.title: The Opening Dispute: Whether Eating a Tiny Bit While Under an Oath Not to Eat Constitutes a Transgression
Rabbi Akiva's Position: A Tiny Bit Suffices for Transgression Because the Violation Is of Words, Not of the Physical Act of Eating
The Rabbis' Position: A Kezayit (Olive-Sized Measure) Is Required, Following the Normal Minimum for Eating-Related Prohibitions
The Conceptual Foundation of Rabbi Akiva's View: Oaths Create Obligations Relating to Words, Not Merely to Physical Acts

macro_segments.title: Investigating Whether Rabbi Akiva Generally Holds That Any Measure Violates Eating Prohibitions or Only for Oaths

micro_segments.title: The Initial Inquiry: Does Rabbi Akiva Hold the Position That Even a Tiny Bit Violates Forbidden Foods Throughout the Torah Like Rabbi Shimon?
Rabbi Shimon's Doctrine: A Tiny Bit of Forbidden Food Incurs Lashes, Though a Kezayit Is Required for an Offering
The Logical Inference: If Rabbi Akiva Holds Shimon's View Generally, He Could Simply Declare That Any Measure Violates All Prohibited Eatings
The Limitation Strategy: The Debate Is Unique to Oaths Alone, Demonstrating the Rabbis' Extreme Position on This Special Category

macro_segments.title: Textual Evidence: The Nazirite Case and Other Comparisons Establishing That a Kezayit Is Normally Required

micro_segments.title: The Come and Hear Statement: Rabbi Akiva's Own Case of a Nazirite Combining Wine and Bread to Reach a Kezayit Measure
The Inference: If Rabbi Akiva Required a Kezayit in the Nazirite Case, He Cannot Hold Generally That Any Measure Suffices
The Conclusion: Rabbi Akiva Must Hold That Kezayit Is Standard Even for Eating Prohibitions, Making His Oath Position Unique

macro_segments.title: The Mishna on Oaths Regarding Already-Forbidden Foods: Can an Oath Take Effect on What Is Biblically Prohibited?

micro_segments.title: The Principle at Stake: The Rule That No Prohibition Can Attach to Something Already Prohibited (Ein Issur Chal Al Issur)
The Case: Someone Swears Not to Eat, Then Eats Carrion (Neveilot), Torn Animals (Tereifot), and Unclean Creatures (Shkatzim and Remasim)
The Mishna's Ruling: Despite the Rule, One Is Liable Because One Also Swore Not to Eat Permitted Foods in the Scope of the Oath
Rabbi Shimon's Exemption: The Rule Against Prohibition Upon Prohibition Applies Absolutely, Exempting Liability for Forbidden Foods

macro_segments.title: The Talmudic Resolution: How an Oath Takes Effect on Forbidden Foods Through the Principle of Inclusive Language

micro_segments.title: The First Approach: Including Permitted Things with Forbidden Things in the Scope of the Oath Allows the Oath to Take Effect on Both
Resh Lakish's Alternative: The Oath Takes Effect If One Specifies a Minimal Measure or According to Rabbi Akiva's Position That Even Any Measure Suffices
Rabbi Akiva's Unique Position: Since He Holds That an Oath Creates Binding Effect Even for a Tiny Bit, the Oath Takes Effect Even on Biblically Forbidden Foods
The Proof of Rabbi Akiva's Position: If He Generally Held Like Rabbi Shimon, the Entire Argument Would Be Moot Because All Forbidden Foods Would Already Violate for Any Measure

macro_segments.title: The Nature of Violations in Oaths vs. Vows: Why Oaths Require No Minimum Measure While Vows and Normal Eating Prohibitions Do

micro_segments.title: The Comparison: Oaths Concern the Violation of One's Word, While Normal Eating Prohibitions and Vows Concern the Act of Eating Itself
The Examples of Qualitative Significance: A Whole Creature (Biryah) Like an Ant or Explicitly Specifying a Tiny Measure Both Give Qualitative Weight to Small Amounts
The Distinction for Oaths: Because the Violation Is of the Spoken Word Rather Than the Physical Act, Any Minute Amount Constitutes Falsifying One's Statement

macro_segments.title: The Nature of Violations in Oaths vs. Vows: Why Oaths Require No Minimum Measure While Vows and Normal Eating Prohibitions Do (continued)

micro_segments.title: The Related Case of Konamos (Vows): Similar to Oaths in Requiring No Minimum, Since the Violation Is of the Status Given to the Object, Not of Eating Per Se

macro_segments.title: Does One Bring an Offering for a False Oath? The Analogy to Me'ila and the Debate Over Whether Vows Are Like Consecrated Objects

micro_segments.title: The Question: Are Oaths Special Because They Bring an Offering for a Transgression Committed by Words Alone?
The Counterexamples: Blasphemy and the Nazirite Both Involve Speech Acts, Yet the First Is Only a Violation Without an Offering and the Second Brings an Offering for Completion, Not Violation
The Narrowed Definition: An Offering Is Brought Only When a Speech Act Creates a Personal Prohibition That Is Then Violated
The Me'ila Analogy: Oaths Might Parallel Consecrated Objects (Hekdesh) in Creating a Status That, When Misused, Requires an Offering
The Vow (Konam) Case: Whether Vows Incur Me'ila-Like Liability and Therefore Require an Offering Depends on Whether They Are Considered Like Consecrated Objects
The Conclusion: Vows Do Not Incur Me'ila, So No Offering Is Required; The Debate About Oaths Remains Narrowed to Cases Involving Personal Prohibitions

macro_segments.title: Rava's Refinements: When the Tiny-Measure Rule Applies to Oaths and When It Does Not

micro_segments.title: The Debate Is Limited to Oaths Alone: If One explicitly states a tiny measure, all agree that a tiny measure suffices
The Status Comparison: An Explicit Statement about a tiny measure is like a qualitatively significant unit (Biryah) in giving weight to the small amount
The Case of Tasting: If One swears not to 'taste' rather than 'eat,' the violation occurs with any tiny amount because tasting inherently means a small amount

macro_segments.title: Rava's Second Refinement: The Distinction Between Oaths on Actions and Vows on Objects Regarding Minimum Measures

micro_segments.title: Oaths vs. Konamos (Vows): The Debate About Minimum Measures Applies to Oaths (Shvuot), Not to Vows on Objects
All Agree on Vows: When One Says an Object Is Konamos (Vow), Even a Tiny Bit Violates the Vow Regardless of Whether One Explicitly States It
The Reason: Since Vows Do Not Use the Language of Eating, They Focus on the Object's Status Rather Than the Act, Thus Requiring No Minimum
